Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Problems and hardships are endless; challenges are a constant part of life.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: There are always difficulties in life.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech, social commentary, humor, politics
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Widely used across southern Nigeria including Port Harcourt; reflects communal resilience amid economic and political instability.
First Example Sentence: As dem increase fuel price again, wahala no dey finish for dis kontri.
Second Example Sentence: I just fix my motor yesterday, today e spoil again—wahala no dey finish.
Third Example Sentence: For dis office, wahala no dey finish since new oga come.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase (bare verb + object) functioning as intransitive idiom
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be left behind, to miss out, or to be the least successful.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Popularized in school and street slang; common in Port Harcourt youth discourse.
First Example Sentence: If you no study well, you go carry last for class.
Second Example Sentence: Run fast make you no carry last for race.
Third Example Sentence: All my friends don buy land, I no wan carry last.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To eat to full satisfaction; to enjoy abundance.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in communal eating traditions across the Niger Delta and beyond.
First Example Sentence: For wedding yesterday, we chop belleful sotey we no fit waka well.
Second Example Sentence: Mama cook afang soup, everybody chop belleful.
Third Example Sentence: After fasting, dem chop belleful for evening.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Phrasal verb construction (verb + object + prepositional phrase)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To interfere or involve oneself in an issue.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: To intervene in someone else's issue.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects oral debate culture in markets and community gatherings.
First Example Sentence: No put mouth for matter wey no concern you.
Second Example Sentence: She like put mouth for everybody quarrel.
Third Example Sentence: Chairman put mouth for issue before e turn big problem.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To experience severe hardship or suffering.
Usage Contexts: Economic hardship narratives, migration stories, humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Originates from Hausa loanword 'shege'; gained popularity during economic downturns in the 1980s.
First Example Sentence: For that contract work, I see shege tire.
Second Example Sentence: When rain start beat us for road, we see shege.
Third Example Sentence: If you no prepare well, you go see shege for exam hall.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative copular clause (NOUN + NEG + copula complement)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The body needs rest and cannot endure unlimited stress.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: You must rest because your body has limits.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common proverb-like expression in southern Nigeria emphasizing human vulnerability.
First Example Sentence: Rest small, body no be firewood.
Second Example Sentence: You dey hustle day and night—body no be firewood o.
Third Example Sentence: After three shifts back to back, I remember say body no be firewood.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative identity clause (NOUN + NEG + copula + noun)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Humans are not perfect and do not have ultimate power.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: No one is all-powerful or perfect.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Influenced by strong Christian and Islamic presence in the Niger Delta region.
First Example Sentence: Forgive am, man no be God.
Second Example Sentence: We fit try our best, but man no be God.
Third Example Sentence: Even doctor fit make mistake—man no be God.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak overly complex English to impress or confuse others.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Emerged from colonial and postcolonial language hierarchies privileging English over indigenous speech.
First Example Sentence: No blow grammar for here, talk wetin you mean.
Second Example Sentence: Anytime dem invite am, e go blow grammar tire.
Third Example Sentence: Politician blow grammar but nobody understand am.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Taking turns in sequence; rotating opportunity.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used prominently in Nigerian political discourse referring to power rotation.
First Example Sentence: Make we answer question turn by turn.
Second Example Sentence: For junction, driver dem dey pass turn by turn.
Third Example Sentence: Dem say leadership na turn by turn this time.

Idiom (Cham): Cut coat according to your size
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Live within your means; avoid overspending.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: Spend only what you can afford.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Adapted from English proverb but localized in Pidgin pragmatics and economic realities.
First Example Sentence: My brother, cut coat according to your size before debt choke you.
Second Example Sentence: As business small like this, we go cut coat according to our size.
Third Example Sentence: She learn early to cut coat according to her size.
